title: Avery Johansson — Ops Advisor, Anchor Codec
compiled_truth:
Avery Johansson is an operations advisor to [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor), the deterministic data infrastructure startup building single-pass primitives for compression, authentication, and transmission. Avery's involvement with the company dates back to early 2026, when the founding team was still figuring out how to stand up a financial backbone that could scale with the product ambitions they had in mind.

Before advising Anchor, Avery served as COO at two high-growth SaaS companies — one in workforce analytics, another in compliance automation. Both experiences gave them a sharp instinct for building operational systems that don't buckle under rapid headcount growth or shifting go-to-market motions. They're known for being deeply collaborative; the kind of operator who sits in the room with engineering leads and finance alike, refusing to let functional silos calcify. People who've worked with Avery describe them as a systems builder first — someone who thinks in feedback loops rather than org charts.

At [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor), Avery's fingerprints are on the financial and operational infrastructure that underpins the company's day-to-day. This includes the budgeting cadence, vendor management framework, and the internal reporting stack that the leadership team uses for quarterly planning. Avery pushed early for a rolling forecast model instead of a traditional annual budget, arguing that a startup moving as fast as Anchor needed shorter planning horizons with tighter review cycles. That recommendation stuck and became one of the defining features of how the company operates internally.

Avery doesn't hold a full-time role — they advise roughly eight to ten hours a month, with heavier involvement during fundraising sprints or when the team is navigating a significant operational inflection. They've been particuarly active during hiring surges, helping design interview loops for ops and finance roles and occasionally sitting in on final-round conversations.

Outside of Anchor, Avery mentors early-stage founders through a couple of informal networks and occasionally speaks at ops-focused events. Terse in meetings, generous with follow-ups — that's the reputation. They tend to send long, detailed memos after calls rather than talking through every nuance in real time. The team has come to rely on those memos as a kind of institutional memory for operational decisions.
timeline:
- **2026-02-14** | First meeting with Anchor Codec founders to discuss operational scaffolding; Avery agreed to advise on a monthly retainer basis.
- **2026-04-03** | Delivered initial memo proposing rolling forecast model and vendor management framework for [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor).
- **2026-07-19** | Helped design the interview loop for Anchor's first dedicated finance hire.
- **2026-11-08** | Participated in board prep sessions ahead of Anchor's seed extension round; reviewed financial narratives and unit economics framing.
- **2027-02-22** | Led an internal ops review with the leadership team, identifying bottlenecks in procurement and contractor onboarding.
- **2027-06-10** | Spoke on a panel about scaling ops infrastructure at early-stage companies; referenced lessons from [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) without naming the company directly.
- **2027-09-15** | Advised on restructuring the quarterly planning cadence after Anchor's headcount crossed 40 employees.
- **2028-01-11** | Joined a two-day offsite with the Anchor leadership team to map out operational priorities for the year.
- **2028-05-03** | Helped evaluate and negotiate Anchor's first enterprise ERP integration, consolidating finance tooling onto a single platform.

_facts.name: Avery Johansson
_facts.title: Ops Advisor
_facts.background: COO at two high-growth SaaS companies; helped Anchor design its financial and operational infrastructure
